6pahpson.name: Social Neuroscience
6pahpson.information:
This course examines the neural mechanisms underlying human social behaviour.  It draws on methods from cognitive neuroscience and research questions from social psychology to address fundamental aspects of how people interact with each other.  Students will develop their knowledge and understanding of: 
• The ways in which neuroscience methods can help to answer questions about social behaviour 
• The cognitive and neural mechanisms that underlie social behaviour and how these may be 
affected in disorders of social interaction 
• Controversies in social neuroscience 
• How scientific research is (mis)represented in the media
6pahpson.assessment: 1000-word Structured Media Critique (30%), 2000 word Study Proposal (70%)

6PAHPSPE.name: The Psychology of Suggestion: Placebo Effects and Beyond
6PAHPSPE.information: In this module, students will learn about research on, and applications of, suggestion effects in a variety of different fields spanning psychology and psychiatry. Students will develop knowledge of the methods used to study, use, and control for suggestion effects in various contexts (e.g., therapeutic interventions and experimental psychology paradigms). They will also develop a comprehensive understanding of contemporary theoretical debates regarding the psychological and neurophysiological mechanisms subserving suggestion effects as well as the role of suggestion in different psychological, psychiatric, medical phenomena. Through a critical review of this domain, students will gain a deeper understanding of the importance of studying and accounting for suggestion effects (and related phenomena) in a variety of research and clinical domains.

6PAHPLER.name: Lived Experience in Mental Health Research
6PAHPLER.module_leaders: Dr Angela Sweeney & Dr Una Foye
6PAHPLER.information:
This module is designed and delivered by researchers with lived experience. We aim to create a lively module that covers a range of contemporary topics including: lived experience, oppression and social justice; lived experience in the Global South; survivor research; Patient and Public Involvement in 
research; ethics; and emotional labour. Later in the module, we will explore the relationship between research, activism, and the arts. The final week will be dedicated to supporting students with the assessment. Learning will be supported by a mixture of lectures and interactive seminars, with time to discuss key issues and consider the assessment. The module will also have weekly hybrid drop-in sessions and an online forum. Students will be asked to keep a light-touch reflective journal, and will be able to dip into an (optional) playlist of music, films and books.
6PAHPLER.assessment:
Assessment will be through multiple choice quizzes (MCQs, 10%) and a 2500 word essay (90%). There will be three MCQs, each with 9 questions. The essay will ask students to write about and reflect on Patient and Public Involvement and research ethics, as well as reflecting on a wider learning point 
from the module.

6PAHPMNE.name: Applied Forensic Psychology
6PAHPMNE.information:
This module will give students an understanding of the role psychology plays in a variety of forensic 
settings, including prisons, secure units, and community settings.  The module will critically evaluate 
how to define and measure the construct of crime and will examine the main psychological theories 
and models that have contributed to our understanding of criminality. Given the applied focus of 
the module, there will be an emphasis on the assessment and treatment of offenders. The module 
explores how clinical and forensic psychologists work as scientist-practitioners and reflective
practitioners in a variety of challenging environments. 
The module is ideally suited to those students who wish to pursue post-graduate studies in the fields 
of clinical and/or forensic psychology. Teaching will consist of one-hour lectures followed by one
hour seminars, with approximately six weeks of teaching being delivered by practising clinical 
and/or forensic psychologists. 
Teaching topics will include developmental and life-course theories of criminality, violent offending 
and mental illness, sexual offending and mental illness, actuarial and structured clinical judgement 
risk assessment, and assessment and interventions with offenders in prison and other secure 
settings.

6PAHPJDM.name: Clinical, Forensic and Managerial Application of Decision Making
6PAHPJDM.information:
The core of this module will build a foundational knowledge of judgement and choice, exploring 
key phenomena that have been identified in research (both laboratory and field) on judgement 
and choice. This includes but is not limited to: the use of heuristics in judgement and decision 
making, perceptions of risk, and influences of emotions. In discussing these, we will examine 
how the key phenomena identified in JDM research has been applied to understand the decision 
making of clinicians and patients, law enforcement, legal professionals and lay jurors, as well 
as business leaders and consumers.  

Our second goal is to allow you to leave the module with a toolbox of interventions that you can 
apply in your future personal and professional decision making. 

Building on your growing foundational knowledge of judgement and choices, this topic of the 
course will explore how we can develop strategies to improve judgements and decision making 
across the clinical, forensic and professional domains, and introduce students to different 
approaches and types of behavioural interventions that target our judgement and decision
making process.

The module makes use of a variety of approaches to learning, including lectures and seminars.

6PAHPART.name: The Psychology of Creativity: Experiencing and Creating the Arts
6PAHPART.information:
The module aims to introduce students to a psychological analysis of the creative arts. Students will
gain a critical appreciation of biological, psychological and individual factors in appreciating art,
creating art, and of the relationship between art and mental health. This will be disseminated
through lectures covering theory and research, and small group seminars and practical activities.

At the end of this module, students will be able to:
• Have a critical understanding of the role of biological and psychological processes in
creativity, and how they interact.
• Will be able to determine the relationship between creativity and mental illness, and the
effectiveness of the arts in therapy
• Have a critical understanding of how individual and social factors impact on creativity, and
in turn how creative arts can shape individual identity.
• Evaluate the interaction of biological, psychological and social/ individual factors in
creativity.
• To be able to critically evaluate relevant theory and research, and present this in a
comparative context in a variety of formats.

The module looks largely at music and visual arts, focusing on three broad strands: appreciation and
perception of art, creation of art, and art and mental health. These will be examined from three
perspectives: biological (neurological bases), psychological (sensory and cognitive processing), and
individual (identity, group membership and personality). Teaching will take place over 10 weeks;
there are online recorded lectures (but some lectures may take place in-person), covering relevant
theory and research, followed by a 1- hour seminar looking at further development of the lecture
topic. For some of the seminars we will be visiting galleries and other relevant locations. Lectures
are split into 3-week blocks which cover: structures and processes; art and the individual; and
practical uses of the arts in psychology. There will be two assessment activities. The first is a
summative portfolio, requiring students to use their own choice of artwork to explore the
relationship between psychology and artistic creativity. The second piece of work is a group
presentation (delivered as a narrated PowerPoint with the option for live presentation) presenting
any topic from either lectures or seminars as an introduction to the topic for users of a community
arts project. There will also be three formative MCQs to allow students to self-check understanding.
Lecture topics include creativity and the brain; music and memory; creativity and humour; musical
taste and social identity.
6PAHPART.assessment: Portfolio (60%), Presentation for a lay audience (40%)

5PAHPIGA.name: Intermediate Graduate Attributes
5PAHPIGA.information:
You have now managed to complete the main transition period in your degree, from school to
university study. However, you are now gearing up to become a graduate in your discipline and
this means more than understanding psychology. The term graduate embodies a range of
qualities. This module is designed to help you in your next step on the journey to becoming a
graduate. The module is not credit-bearing so it will not contribute to your overall degree
result, but it will show up on your academic transcript, a document often requested by
employers. This will allow you to demonstrate to future employers that your skills extend well
beyond your discipline specific knowledge, something that is increasingly important in a
competitive workplace. 

This module provides you with an opportunity to develop key graduate attributes in a way
that complements your other modules within the BSc Psychology degree and supports
personal development. You are provided with weekly learning activities online, either
asynchronously trough LinkedIn Learning, or synchronously through MS Teams. These
activities will fall into the following categories:
• Core Study Skills e.g., note-taking and reading skills
• Communication Skills e.g., writing and presenting
• Planning, Projects, and People e.g., working in groups and time management
• Skills for Mental Wellbeing e.g., mindfulness, dealing with failure
• Being Tech Savvy e.g., internet safety, using Word and PowerPoint
• Building Your Career e.g., looking at summer work opportunities
5PAHPIGA.assessment: Determined by the number of activities completed.

6PAHPAGA.name: Advanced Graduate Attributes
6PAHPAGA.information:
This optional module provides students with an opportunity to develop key graduate attributes in
manner which integrates with the core BSc Psychology modules and supports their personal
development.
Students completing 6PAHPAGA can develop skills in a number of key areas important for their
transition graduate employment including:
• Career planning
• Project management
• Time management
• Networking
• Social media branding
• Job interviewing, pitches and questioning

At the end of this module, students will:
• Have developed graduate skills such as project management and goal setting.
• Be aware of the challenges in graduate job hunting and how to prepare for these.
• Be able to support their wellbeing in their career.

Students are provided with weekly learning activities either online or face to face (22 activities
available in total). These activities will fall into the following categories:
• Core Study Skills,
• Communication Skills,
• Planning, Projects and People,
• Skills for Mental Wellbeing,
• Being Tech Savvy,
• Building Your Career
Students can select a minimum of four activities to complete during the year to pass this module.
Completion of activities is indicated by attendance at face-to-face learning activities or completion
of an online activity. All online activities are delivered via KEATs and via Lynda.com (a learning system
recently purchased by the University) and students will have the opportunity to complete on campus
with staff support as well as remotely.
6PAHPAGA.assessment:
Participation and engagement demonstrated via attendance at workshops (typically in person) or via
online logs and completion of KEATS quizzes.

5PAHPIDP.name: The Origins of Individual Differences
5PAHPIDP.module_leaders: Dr Lucy Livingston & Dr Maria Livanou
5PAHPIDP.information:
This module builds upon the material taught in the first-year modules, allowing you to
appreciate how the material from the different psychological disciplines to which you have
been exposed can be integrated to study how differences between individuals arise.
The module will examine the classical distinction between ‘nature’ and ‘nurture’, and the
status of genetic and environmental explanations of psychological variation. The research
techniques that will be covered include twin studies, gene-environment interaction studies,
Genome Wide Association Studies (GWAS) and polygenic risk scores.
The module will also explore the nature of gender differences, including the role of biological
sex and culture in the development of gender identity, and sex differences in cognitive
abilities and vulnerability to mental health problems and developmental conditions such as
autism.
Lastly, the module will explore social and cultural influences as well as the role of childhood
trauma on the vulnerability to mental health problems, using developmental psychology,
global mental health and social psychiatry approaches.

5PAHPGPI.name: Perspectives on Global Poverty & Inequality
5PAHPGPI.information:
This module will familiarise you with contemporary psychological and interdisciplinary
perspectives on poverty and social inequalities. Building upon the first-year core module
4PAHPSOP (Psychology and Society), you will explore a diverse range of topics
concerning the causes and consequences of inequalities. Drawing upon insights from
sociology, economics and anthropology, you will examine the socio-cultural and political
embeddedness of social problems and their impact on individuals and groups. The
module encompasses various research traditions, theoretical frameworks and empirical
perspectives, whilst considering both British and global contexts. You will develop skills
in critically evaluating evidence on these topics, analysing both methodological
approaches and overall scientific rigour.

5PAHPSHP.name: Distorted Worlds: Variability in Memory and Perception
5PAHPSHP.module_leaders: Dr Samuel Westwood & Dr Devin Terhune
5PAHPSHP.information:
This module aims to build upon the material taught in the first-year modules to allow
students to appreciate how the material from the various domains of psychological
enquiry can be integrated to study how we represent the world.
The module will be taught via a series of lectures and small group seminars (where issues
raised in the lectures will be covered in greater depth) and then material will be further
consolidated and advanced through practical work. The seminars and practical sessions
will provide an interactive environment to encourage a deep approach to learning.
The module will explore the concept of variation in perception and memory in both
typical and atypical functioning. Topics will include: the operation of the visual system;
how perceptual illusions illustrate perceptual mechanisms; the development and
construction of a sense of self; and the nature of distorted perception and attention in
disease, including neuropsychological conditions such as neglect and psychotic illness.
Students will also study the properties of memory, including insights from
psychobiology, neuropsychological patients with acquired amnesia, the implications of
the reconstructive nature of episodic memory and social and cognitive studies of
memory distortions, e.g., misinformation effects and the role of interference in long-term
memory.

6PAHPADD.name: Drug Use & Addiction
6PAHPADD.information:
This module will provide students with an in-depth understanding of the psychopharmacological
effects of various recreational drugs, neuroscientific and psychological theories that aim to explain
addiction, and contemporary controversiesissues in addiction science and its application in the realworld. Students will learn about the various psychological and pharmacological treatments for
addiction. A big emphasis will be put on integrating evidence from different disciplines and
evaluating the strength of different forms of evidence. Topics covered are: theories of addiction,
nicotine & tobacco, alcohol, cannabis, opioids, psychedelics & ketamine, psychosocial treatments
for addiction, women & drug use, drug policy, and animal models in addiction.

At the end of this module, you should be able to:
• Understand and evaluate key theories and evidence regarding the aetiology and maintenance
of substance use disorders
• Understand historical and current conceptualisations of substance use disorder and
diagnostic processes
• Understand the current (and historical) UK and global prevalence rates of substance use and
substance use disorders
• Understand the pharmacological properties and psychiatric effects of a range of recreational
drugs (e.g., cannabis, opioids), including their primary mechanisms of action and associations
with chronic neurobiological changes
• Understand and explain current strategies for treatment of substance use disorder, including
psychosocial and pharmacological interventions, and the evidence regarding their effectiveness
• Recognise and critically discuss contemporary issues and areas of contention within the drug
use field, including controversies surrounding the aetiology of addiction, legalisation of
recreational drugs, and harm reduction strategies
• Understand and evaluate the implications of addiction research on policy and practice,
including strategies for the treatment of substance use disorder and the regulation of
substances
 • Describe, analyse, evaluate and apply psychopharmacology, drug use, and addiction research
effectively in an oral presentation, exam quiz questions, and an exam essay.

This module builds on the second half of Level 5 Choices module, providing an optional module for
students wishing to further their understanding of psychopharmacology, substance use and
addiction. Through 10 weekly in-person lectures and seminars, students will be given a background
in a variety of disciplines related to drug use and addiction science. Seminars include interactive
debates, roleplay, presentations, a participatory coffee vs. decaf RCT workshop, a mock exam, and
feedback on the mock exam. The module’s learning outcomes are assessed with a small-group oral
presentation (10 mins) and a closed-book exam that requires one essay to be written and quiz
questions to be answered (2 hours). The module lead provides supportive formative work and a
mock exam to support both assessments.
6PAHPADD.assessment:
Small-Group 10-minute Oral Presentation (30%), Closed-Book Exam with one Essay and Quiz
Questions (70%)

4PAHPBIO.name: Psychology and the Brain
4PAHPBIO.module_leaders: Dr Stacey Bedwell, Dr Ashley Brown & Prof Ellie Dommett
4PAHPBIO.information:
The aim of this module is to introduce you to the key principles of neuroscience that underpin
our understanding of behaviour in biological psychology. This module will provide you with
introductory knowledge of the nervous system, including divisions, development, and cell
signalling. You will also learn about different methods for investigating the brain. You will be
introduced to specific functions of the brain, including perception, attention, memory, and
language. You will learn about neurogenerative diseases and psychiatric disorders. Across the
ten-week term, you will cover nine topics and have a week to consolidate your learning and
build links between topics in the final teaching week. The module will be taught via a series
of lectures, seminars and practical classes.

5PAHPCLN.name: Clinical Psychology
5PAHPCLN.module_leaders: Dr James Findon & Dr Ashley Brown
5PAHPCLN.information:
The course aims to develop a critical appreciation of methodological issues in studying mental
ill-health and wellbeing. Students, upon taking this module, should develop an appreciation
of the role of psychology in understanding and alleviating mental health difficulties and
promoting wellbeing across mental health conditions such as depression, psychosis, anxiety
disorders and eating disorders.
5PAHPCLN.assessment:
This module is assessed through both coursework and in person quizzes. The quizzes
contribute 40% (30% individual quiz and 10% team quiz).
The coursework is made up of an essay (30%) and group video presentation (30%).

5PAHPRM3.name: Research Methods 3
5PAHPRM3.module_leaders: Prof. Mike Aitken-Deakin  & Dr Ashely Brown
5PAHPRM3.information:
This module continues the discussion of statistical analyses we started last in year RM1 and RM2. The aim is to deepen your understanding of the material covered in these modules including inference about of sample means and linear correlation and extend these techniques to more complex analysis (with multiple different groups, multiple manipulations, or multiple types of measurement). 
Much of what we will do will be based around introducing the most common form of inferential statistical analysis (Analysis of Variance) and related techniques. This module will introduce the theory and develop practical knowledge of how to do all these types of analysis, which will form the basis of the module assessment. Module 5PAHPRM4 will consolidate and extend much of this material, including broader types of application. 
Educational aims of module
This module builds on the Research Methods modules in Year 1. Topics are introduced via brief lecture demonstrations, along with video explanations, detailed notes and examples. There are weekly computer classes where students can extend their knowledge of statistical analysis techniques, including the practical aspects of performing these analyses using SPSS or JASP. In addition to consolidating material already covered in year one, students will learn the theory and application of analysis of variance including issues around multiple comparisons, analysis of repeated measures and factorial designs, and be introduced to multiple regression and analysis of covariance models. Students will develop the practical skills required to perform and interpret these types of analysis on real-world data sets.
In addition, there will a couple of on-campus lectures a series of seminars, students will develop their understanding of the principles of hypothesis-testing, research design, including planning for the use of factorial designs, and using statistical power analysis to determine appropriate sample sizes for research.

4PAHPRM1.name: Research Methods 1
4PAHPRM1.module_leaders: Dr Tim Rakow & Dr Dan Kennedy-Higgins
4PAHPRM1.information:
Psychologists are interested in understanding and explaining human behaviour. To acquire new insights into behaviour, research psychologists conduct studies that involve some form of systematic observation
and measurement of people’s behaviour. Almost always, this involves measuring the behaviour of several individuals – often, in more than one group of participants, or with research participants performing a task
under different sets of circumstances (‘under different conditions’). These studies produce data: information that can be used to draw conclusions. The data can take different forms though, often, we
have quantitative (numeric) data. For instance, we may have categorical data summarised as frequencies or ‘counts’ (e.g., how many people performed a particular behaviour) or we may have measurement data (e.g., scores representing how much, how quickly or how well each individual in a study performed a specified behaviour). To understand the behaviour investigated by a study, we need to examine the data generated by the study and draw reasonable conclusions from the data. Learning about research methods will equip you to understand this process that starts with designing a study and collecting data, through to analysing the data, drawing conclusions, and reporting the research. In your degree, we aim to help you become a critical consumer of psychological research, so that you can identify what can, or cannot, be
concluded from a research study. Another key aim is to give you tools and skills for conducting and reporting psychological research.

In this module, you will learn about the key principles that underpin the design, implementation and interpretation of research studies about human behaviour. You will also be taught a range of statistical techniques and learn how to interpret the information that these techniques produce, so that you are
better able to understand and interpret psychological research conducted by others and are equipped to
conduct psychological research and to draw appropriate conclusions from this research.

4PAHPADD.name: Addressing Problem
4PAHPADD.module_leaders: Dr Tim Rakow & Dr Sumeyra Yalcintas
4PAHPADD.information:
This module complements your teaching on your other Year 1 modules, in particular, the following four modules: Psychology and Society, Psychology and the Brain, Psychology and the Individual, and Psychology and Development. The focus of this module is applying the knowledge you learn in these modules to complex real-world scenarios. 
You will work in small groups throughout the year to develop a project which applies psychology to a specific problem within a set scenario. At the end of each term, you will present your group project through a poster (Term 1) or audio-visual presentation (Term 2). 
You will have considerable flexibility in choosing the topics to address within the set scenario, which is provided at the start of each term.
In addition to your group presentation, you will submit a Critical Evaluation Portfolio at the end of each term. This Portfolio will include several pieces of writing in which you reflect on how you approached your group work activities, explain and evaluate the development of your group’s project, and discuss what you have learned about applying psychological knowledge to address real-world problems.
Educational aims of module
This module aims to provide you with an opportunity to apply knowledge from other core modules to real-world situations, and to develop transferable skills, including team-working, project- and time-management, IT and presentation skills.
Through exposure to real-world issues, you will see the many ways in which Psychology, combined with other disciplines, can make a significant contribution based on research and its application to complex situations. 
You will work to develop your teamwork skills through collaborative activity and develop skills for oral and visual presentation.

6PAHPUAE.name: The Use and Abuse of Evidence in Psychology
6PAHPUAE.information:
Built around four workstreams, plus a series of activities that support all of the workstreams, this
module uses a range of types of learning activity to explore and understand the status of the
evidence presented by research psychologists, and how that evidence is presented and used
outside the domain of psychological research.
The first workstream (understanding evidence) builds on your understanding of research methods
and statistics, and core knowledge of psychology, from Level 4 and 5, to gain a deeper
understanding of how evidence is generated by the collection, analysis and interpretation of
quantitative data, and how research evidence is presented to undergraduate students. In
particular, we focus on what can and can(not) be concluded from research data, and consider
common errors of interpretation and their consequences. This will give you some tools to evaluate
the work of psychology researchers and educators. This workstream will include interactive
lectures, workshops and directed reading.

A second workstream (handling evidence) focuses on the dissemination and transmission of
research findings and their interpretation. In particular, we examine chains of communication
(e.g., from research to article, to press release, to newspaper report) to examine how and why the
meaning of a piece of research can become distorted as it moves along that chain. This
workstream will include interactive lectures, workshops and directed reading.
A third workstream (communicating evidence) examines ‘popular’ science writing on psychology,
such as ‘self-help’ literature or general interest reading for personal curiosity. You will read a book
from this genre; and will participate in weekly or bi-weekly ‘book group’ discussions with others
reading the same book. The discussion activities will help you to apply your knowledge of
psychology and psychological research to undertake and present in-depth analysis of the ideas
and arguments presented in the book.
A fourth workstream (applying evidence) examines how psychological evidence is used in the
public interest. We will examine cases where psychologists seek to put research evidence or
evidence-informed advice into the hands of the general public, with a view to enhancing individual
wellbeing and/or societal benefit. We examine and evaluate how this has been done; and what
means are effective for communicating evidence for psychological research. This project will include workshops and student presentations
6PAHPUAE.assessment: Coursework portfolio (50%), In-depth review or report (50%)

6PAHPRES.name: Research Project
6PAHPRES.module_leaders: Dr Rebecca Upsher & Dr Claire Heard & Dr Alan Meehan
6PAHPRES.information:
6PAHPRES is a compulsory module and is worth 45 credits, i.e. equivalent to three ‘standard’
modules.
Your final-year research project is the culmination of your BSc Psychology journey and will bring
together the skills you have gained in many of your previous modules. You will gain a thorough
understanding of the research process by planning and carrying out your own research project,
which will give you the chance to contribute to the production of new knowledge in an area of
Psychology of your choice.
The final-year project is the single most important coursework submission, with the module
accounting for 37.5% of the final-year mark. It represents your opportunity to make a unique
contribution to scientific knowledge by undertaking a piece of novel empirical psychology research,
under supervision from an expert researcher. The project allows you to experience the excitement
of leading an investigation into an area of psychology of your choice and draw on aspects of your
learning from across the programme to stimulate and enrich this work. The project offers many
opportunities to develop and apply core research skills. You will be required to: design the study
aims, research questions and/or hypotheses; plan an appropriate investigation; recruit participants
where appropriate; conduct psychological or related tests or measures; enter and manage data;
conduct appropriate statistical or qualitative analyses
6PAHPRES.assessment:
Project Registration (5%), Poster Presentation (15%), 6000-word Project Report (75%), Supervisor
Report (5%)

4PAHPDFF.name: Psychology and the Individual
4PAHPDFF.module_leaders: Dr Rebecca Upsher & Dr Salim Hashmi
4PAHPDFF.information:
Psychology and the Individual is an exploration of how and why individuals differ in their
thoughts, emotions, and behaviours. This module delves into the study of personality and
intelligence, examining how these traits develop, how they are measured, and how they
influence behaviour. It incorporates key theoretical models, historical perspectives, and
contemporary debates in differential psychology. The module also introduces behavioural
psychology and learning theories, addressing the impact of life experiences on shaping
individual differences. Through a multidisciplinary lens, this module encourages critical
evaluation of the genetic and environmental contributions to personality and intelligence,
offering a comprehensive understanding of individual variation.
Educational aims of module
Through this module we will explore personality and intelligence and consider different
models for explaining and predicting behaviour. We will address what personality is, how
personality develops and what behaviour we can predict from personality. We will consider
the same questions for intelligence. The module will introduce you to learning theory and
ideas of behavioural psychology – we will consider how our life experience shapes who we
are today.

5PAHKSCS.name: Key Studies in Cognitive Science
5PAHKSCS.module_leaders: Dr Dan Kennedy-Higgins & Dr Julia Ouzia
5PAHKSCS.information:
In this module led by Dan Kennedy-Higgins and Julia Ouzia, students will learn how central
questions in Cognitive Science have historically been approached and will be exposed to the
range of experimental paradigms that were used when attempting to answer those central
questions. For example, we will consider a series of questions such as: 'how do we recognise
what one person is saying when others are speaking at the same time?'; 'how do we know
when we are moving as opposed to our environment? and why is it sometimes difficult to tell,
for example, when sat on a train?’ and 'what causes us to forget information?'. By
understanding the answers to each of these questions (and others) you will gain a deeper
understanding of cognitive science; the role of attention, perception and sensation in our
everyday lives; and the importance of careful experimental manipulation and data collection
as a part of understanding and explaining fundamental cognitive processes.

This module will provide students with a comprehensive overview of some of the most
important questions and findings in Cognitive Science. Each week we will consider a different
question about cognition, provide the students with an in-depth insight into how these
fundamental questions have historically been approached, and discuss what can be learned
from such approaches.
The module adds to knowledge gained in the Research Methods modules, by increasing
students’ understanding of techniques in experimental cognitive science; In addition, the module will broaden understanding of Cognitive Science introduced in 4PAHPBIO Psychology
and the Brain, 5PAHPSHP Distorted Worlds and 5PAHPGEN Choices: Agency, Autonomy and
Addiction.
5PAHKSCS.assessment: Critical review essay (60%) and audio-visual group presentation (40%)

6PAHSCAP.name: Sociocultural Approaches to Psychology, Society and Mental Health
6PAHSCAP.information:
Psychology is a divided discipline, and even the sub-discipline of social psychology manages to feature
a wide range of approaches, not all of them compatible with one another in terms of epistemology
and methodology, although they often sit side-by-side and unquestioned in textbooks. This module
will focus on the sociocultural approaches to psychology which have arisen in part as a result of the
crisis in social psychology in the 1960s/70s, when the subject matter and purpose of the discipline
was hotly contested. Approaches such as social representations theory were developed as a direct
challenge to what was seen as an overly individualised focus which had, effectively, 'written out' the
social.
Taught over one semester, this module will feature ten three-hour sessions. Roughly the first 1.5
hours of each session will be a lecture, while the second will take the form of a seminar focused on
discussion of a particular paper or topic. Sessions will consider the background and context of the
development of these approaches, before thoroughly addressing the theoretical and methodological
aspects of them. Studies that have drawn on these sociocultural approaches will then be considered
in more detail: many of those used will relate in particular to the topic of mental health, but we will
also cover health more broadly, as well as issues including identity, climate change and ageing. Some
sessions will be delivered by sociocultural psychologists working specifically on these topics.
There will be two assessments. The first will be a group presentation: this will aim to assess learning
objectives regarding appreciation of the challenges and contested issues within the area, and to fully
cover the reasons for these. Students who wish to can opt to submit a recorded individual
presentation instead of participating in the group presentation. This will take place at the end of the
sessions that deal with the background and theory of sociocultural psychology. The second will be
one 2500 word assignment, which will be due at the end of the module. Students will be given a choice
of papers describing studies that have a taken a sociocultural approach; they will be required to
choose one, and will be asked to discuss the reasons for the choice of this approach, the contribution
it makes to the literature, and the strengths and weaknesses of this.

4PAHPDEV.module_leaders: Dr Salim Hashmi (salim.hashmi@kcl.ac.uk)                Dr Alan Meehan (alan.meehan@kcl.ac.uk)       Dr Sumeyra Yalcintas (sumeyra.yalcintas@kcl.ac.uk)
4PAHPDEV.information: Developmental psychology is the scientific study of how and why human beings change over the course of their life. Originally concerned with infants and children, the field has expanded to include adolescence, adult development, aging, and the entire lifespan. Developmental psychology is a multidisciplinary framework, with much attention being paid to the distinction between ‘nature’ and ‘nurture’ and the status genetic and environmental explanations for psychological development.
4PAHPDEV.assessment: One 1500-word coursework and a 50 multiple choice questions exam

4PAHPISP.module_leaders: Dr Alan Meehan & Dr Stacey Bedwell
4PAHPISP.information:
This module complements the other seven Year 1 modules to offer students insight into the research activity taking place at the Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology & Neuroscience 
(IoPPN), and allow them to explore their own ideas, develop their research reading skills, and finally develop a detailed funding proposal based on what they have learned. 
Educational aims of module 
This module aims to expose students to a range of world-leading research at the IoPPN, both within and outside Psychology, in two strands: 
Strand 1 is the Research Methods Carousel. These are ‘hands on’ demonstrations of the full 
range of experimental and therapeutic methods conducted at the IoPPN. Each session requires some preparation in advance using materials available on KEATS. There is also an opportunity to learn some key research principles from the perspective of a participant. 
Strand 2 are Researcher Q&A sessions presented by researchers at various career stages who are at the forefront of their fields. As well as hearing about the passion, frustrations, and excitement of research, students will experience research presentations across a range of subjects, and will be encouraged to see potential integrations between different areas.
4PAHPISP.assessment:
1. Reflecting On Research (25% of Module Mark [or 50% w/ sufficient participation credits]) 
2. Reading Research (25% of Module Mark [or 50% w/ sufficient participation credits]) 
3. Using Research (50% of Module Mark [if submitted/insufficient participation credits)

5PAH0333.information: The module, led by Dr Oliver Runswick, will explore the role of Psychology in explaining variation in performance levels in sports and other areas of human performance. The module will consider how levels of performance, including elite performance, might be influenced by psychological concepts including: individual differences (such as in motivation, confidence, or perception), amount and nature of training or practice, effects of competitive stress, and other factors. The module will also describe how techniques based on psychological theories and models may be used in interventions designed to improve performance (including coaching, techniques such as imagery, arousal regulation, and goal setting, and training technologies). Students will be introduced to the evidence base for these interventions and the practicalities and challenges of applying these psychological techniques.  In the coursework assessment you will develop an experiment that measures performance and underpinning psychological mechanisms in any performance task that you are interested in. Previous examples include a wide range of sports, music, dance, emergency services, surgery and others.
5PAH0333.assessment: Written exam (70% of module grade); four-page coursework (30% of module grade)

5PAHPBIO.information:
Educational Aims 

This module, led by Ellie Dommett, aims to provide you with an opportunity to gain knowledge of current applications of neuroscience beyond psychology and biological science and critically evaluate the claims and evidence that arises from these applications. In addition to gaining new knowledge and understanding of the disciplines to which neuroscience contributes, you will develop your group work and presentation skills and digital capabilities, including how to conduct effective database searches, citation searches and how to keep up to date with research literature, which will support you in your third-year project later.  

Module Description 
This module builds on the Level 4 modules within the BSc Psychology, providing an optional module for students wishing to apply the foundational knowledge they gained previously to fields beyond biological psychology and neuroscience. The module will support you in critically evaluating the application of neuroscience to several different disciplines including neuromarketing, neurolaw and neuroeducation. The first half of the module will consist of a series of lectures and seminars to give you a grounding in each topic and support with developing your critical evaluation skills, through group seminar presentations. The second half of the module will allow you to do a deep dive into a topic of your choosing from those taught in the first half, as you work towards producing a critical essay on that topic. The teaching in this second half focuses on developing your digital capabilities and evaluation skills through online learning and in-person journal clubs as well as giving you more time for independent study of your chosen topic. Essay workshops will also be provided.
5PAHPBIO.assessment:
• Seminar presentations worth 20% (4 x peer assessed presentations held in the seminars in Weeks 2-5)  
• Critical essay worth 50% (1300 words)  
• Record and Reflection worth 30% in which you document the process of identifying sources for your 
essay and reflect on this (800 words)

5PAHPCCD.module_leaders: Dr Salim Hashmi & Dr Sumeyra Yalcintas
5PAHPCCD.information:
In this module, led by Dr Salim Hashmi and Dr Sumeyra Yalcintas students will learn how central questions in Developmental Psychology have been approached through both the classic or key studies in the field (i.e., research that originated a seminal developmental theory or phenomenon), and how these questions are approached within contemporary research. Through appreciation and critical reflection on both classic and contemporary approaches to addressing these questions, students will gain a deeper understanding of important concepts in Developmental Science, and the importance of research methodology as a fundamental part of approaching these questions. 
Educational Aims 
• To provide students with a clear insight into some of the classic studies, findings and theories that have been used to address fundamental questions in areas of Developmental Psychology. 
• To consider how the questions addressed by these classic studies are answered using contemporary methods, with reference to recent research in Developmental Science. 
• To compare the classic and contemporary research methods, and critically evaluate how they are used to answer fundamental questions in Developmental Psychology. For each week, there will be a pre-recorded lecture that covers in detail classic studies that have been key to a particular area of Developmental Science, discuss what can be learned from such approaches, as well as any relevant critiques. In a live lecture, we will then discuss how contemporary research answers related questions using updated or novel approaches. Finally, in the seminar each week, we will explore how the topic content can be and is communicated to different audiences.
5PAHPCCD.assessment:
Assessment of the module will include a series of online quiz activities assessing learning throughout the 
module and one written piece of coursework: 
• MCQ Quizzes in Weeks 2-10 (9 quizzes in overall contribute 50% of module grade).  
• One piece of coursework, no more than 1500 words in length, in which you will present a study related to one of the module topics written in an appropriate writing style for a specific audience (50% of module grade).

5PAHPMND.information:
This module, led by Dr James Stazicker, explores foundational and methodological questions about psychology and cognitive science, exploiting the tools of philosophy to equip you to reflect critically on assumptions, concepts and methods used in the rest of your degree. In the first half of term, we apply this distinctive approach to five specific topics that may be familiar from other modules: change blindness and inattentional blindness; experimental evidence about conscious choice and free will; unconscious bias and implicit attitudes; the concept of mental disorder; social cognition and mirror neurons. In the second half of term, we investigate more general questions about aims, methods and assumptions in psychology, focussing on these topics: the nature of psychological explanation and its relationship with neuroscience; the analogy between minds and computers; the role of mental representation in psychological processes; the use of neuroimaging to ascribe mental states. 
Educational Aims 
This module aims to give students the intellectual skills required to investigate the topics mentioned above autonomously. It also aims to foster the following abilities in students: to read and analyse texts carefully; to reflect critically on key concepts and assumptions in contemporary psychology and cognitive science; to develop coherent logical arguments; to express contestable ideas clearly, both in writing and in speech.
5PAHPMND.assessment: The module will be assessed by coursework: one 600-word written critical response to a particular article (25%), and one 2,000-word essay (75%).

4PAHPRM2.name: Research Method 2
4PAHPRM2.module_leaders: Dr Samuel Westwood & Dr Dan Kennedy-Higgins
4PAHPRM2.information: Research methods … there is so much fun to be had, you cannot fit it all into one module! You will see from the KEATS pages that Research Methods 2 (RM2) looks a lot like Research Methods 1 (RM1). And, yes, the structure of the learning activities is quite similar for the two modules, and we will continue to learn more about research design (in the principles of research component) and to learn more techniques for analysing psychological data (in the data concepts and data analysis components). It is, however, worth emphasising some of the features of your research training that will take on added importance – and perhaps create greater challenge – in the RM2 module. First, as you gain competence in using an increasing number of data analysis techniques, we are hoping that you will become increasingly independent in applying your data analysis skills. With a large number of statistical ‘tools’ at your disposal, an important (and sometimes challenging) skill is to choose the right tool for a given psychology research question. Confidence and competence in this area is an important part of becoming a good researcher, and will also make you a more discerning ‘consumer’ of psychology research.Second, given that research is how we create new knowledge in psychology, it is important that we can communicate research findings to others. The standard way of doing so is via a research report – following the format that you will have seen in several of the journal articles that you have read. In RM2, we will begin the process of training you to write research reports. You will write two research reports for your RM2 coursework. You should not be surprised if you find this more demanding than the RM1 coursework – because research reports require a wide range of competencies, including good attention to structure and detail, skills in data analysis and interpretation, and clear succinct writing that is packed with content. Therefore, we will take quite a bit of time to look at how you can hone these skills, which will be important throughout your degree (and very likely after you graduate too, if you are working in an area where you use the skills from your psychology degree).We hope that you find the module enjoyable, interesting and rewarding.
4PAHPRM2.assessment: This module is assessed through both coursework and an examination, each of which contributes 50% of your final module grade. To pass RM2, you need to achieve an (overall) aggregate mark of 40 or more for the module. You must pass RM2 before you can commence Year 2 of the BSc Psychology degree.

6PAHBRA.name: Research and Clinical Issues in Women’s Mental Health
6PAHBRA.module_leaders: Dr Sian Oram & Dr Roxanne Keynejad
6PAHBRA.information: Sex and gender differences in the burden of mental illness are well-established, with women experiencing higher prevalence of disorders such as depression and anxiety. Identifying sex and gender specific risk factors and effective interventions for mental illness is of crucial importance, yet vulnerable populations, such as pregnant women with mental illness, are often excluded from research for practical and ethical reasons. This limits the evidence base for policymaking, service delivery, and clinical decision making. This is an optional module within the third year of BSc Psychology. The module provides students with an introduction to epidemiological research methods in the context of mental illness. Students on this course will gain knowledge of sex and gender differences in the prevalence, presentation and prognosis of mental disorders, and the biological and sociocultural influences on these differences. Through a series of lectures and seminars students will be given a grounding and opportunity to engage in key topics including: 1) sex, gender, and mental health; 2) violence against women and mental health; 3) mental health in the perinatal period; 4) ethical and safety issues in women’s mental health research and working with vulnerable populations.
6PAHBRA.assessment: a poster focusing on a critical appraisal of research (30% weighting, submitted partway through the course, please note that the poster is submitted online and there is no presentation component) and a 2,750 word coursework essay focusing on a critical appraisal of research (70% weighting, submitted after the course ends). Poster and coursework essay questions are pre-specified; students select from two options for the poster and for three options for the essay.

6PAHCIAQ.information:
The module will run across one semester and introduce contemporary issues and approaches in 
quantitative methods, such as Open Science principles or Bayesian statistics. Topics will be introduced via online resources (e.g., videos), which will usually cover meta-scientific debates, ethical issues, quantitative approaches, and future directions in the given area of research. This will be followed by independent study of reading materials and other media, such as podcasts. The class will then convene for in-person teaching sessions, which will consist of a mix of seminars, practicals, and journal clubs. In seminars, students will gain practical skills in analysing meta-scientific issues in quantitative research. In practicals, students will gain hands-on experience in some of the research methods introduced in the course. In journal clubs, they will lead critical debates on current issues in quantitative research with reference to a target paper.

6PAHCIDP.information: Students will engage with a series of contemporary topics within developmental psychology that have been the focus of public interest and debate. By considering the historical and theoretical perspectives used to inform thinking around these topics, as well as the novel research methods that have been developed to generate recent evidence on these topics, students will gain a deeper understanding of the extent to which key questions around the development of children and young people have been answered, and critically reflect on wider issues around the importance of appropriate research methodology and scientific quality when seeking to answer these questions. Each week’s lecture material will introduce a current topic or issue, set out the prevailing arguments within the debate around this issue, and outline the theoretical background and models used to support these positions. Additionally, through a combination of seminars and workshops, students will move beyond the lecture content to identify outstanding 'gaps' in the literature and apply different designs and methods to generate ideas for future research directions in developmental psychology, all of which will inform a final piece of summative coursework.

6PAHCIMH.information: In this module students will develop an understanding of the link between culture, identity and mental health. Students will consider different definitions of culture and explore key themes linking culture and mental health. Additionally, they will examine how culture plays a role in the presentation, treatment and assessment of mental health conditions through evaluating current evidence. This module will take a team-based learning approach which requires students to complete preparatory work (which will include watching lecture videos) which they will then apply in workshops. In the seminars the students will further develop their ability to critically reflect through the use of diverse reflective models. These sessions will also support students in the preparation of their coursework.

6PAHCLRE.information: This module provides students with a deep understanding of how close relationships form, develop and end, and most importantly how they affect one's life. Each week we will look at a different type of close relationship, its nature of it and its effects on development and well-being. We will look at how each relationship develops, the main theoretical background and how it influences individuals through their lifespan. By discovering each relationship's nature and its effects, students will appreciate close relationships and their several effects on life-span development. We will critically analyse how different relationships interact together to affect each other and individuals.
6PAHCLRE.assessment: Conference Abstract 500 words (30%) & Project proposal 2000 words (70%)

6PAHENED.information: The Early Neurocognitive Development module provides a unique opportunity to explore cutting-edge research on early-life typical and atypical brain development and potential clinical and policy implications. The module will run across a single semester. In the first half of the module, students will learn the fundamental principles of early development, ranging from language development to social perception. In the second half of the module, students consolidate this learning through contemporary issues in the field, on topics ranging from emerging neurodevelopmental conditions, the effect of sleep, and global perspectives on brain development. Online video resources will be supported by interactive seminar sessions focused on current debates and issues in the field, in addition to hands-on demonstrations of neurocognitive data acquisition and analysis as applied to early development, to provide the necessary skills for study design and critique.
6PAHENED.assessment: Group Lay Narrated Presentation (30%) & Research Proposal (70%)

6PAHNPDM.information:
The module covers decision-making as an executive function. Beginning with the basic neurobiology behind decision making processes, working through various models and theories of decision making and finishing the module by considering wider applications and implications such as decision-making 
deficits and how our decisions are influenced. The module will run across one semester and cover ten topics (see below) through 10 x 1.5 hour lectures, 5 x 2 hour practical classes and 5 x 1.5 hour seminars. The lecture content will usually cover theoretical concepts and research evidence, whilst the practical classes and seminars will provide opportunity to discuss and critique evidence, put into practice some experimental methods and measures, and apply knowledge from the lectures to different scenarios. Seminars are aimed to be more discussion based e.g. critiquing contemporary research evidence and practical classes applying knowledge e.g. using measures of decision-making style. Topics to be covered: • Neuroanatomy in the context of decision-making• Neurodevelopment of decision-making• Importance of the prefrontal cortex• Neurotransmitters and neurophysiology of decision-making• Decision networks• Decision-making styles and models of decision-making• The role of childhood experiences in decision-making• Decision-making deficits
6PAHNPDM.assessment:
The module will be assessed through two elements:  
1.  5x short online quizzes, spread throughout the module, and a single piece of coursework in the form of a video. Each quiz will be comprised of 10 multiple choice questions (totalling 50 questions over the module). The 10 question quizzes will be spaced evenly through the semester e.g. weeks 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10, and will assess content from the previous two weeks e.g. 
week 2 quiz assesses topics covered in weeks 1 and 2. A total grade is given for all 5 quizzes combined. 
2. An educational video aimed at children e.g. in the style of a YouTube video. The students will be asked to create and record a 10-minute video in a creative style and format suitable for the target audience, covering one of three provided key topics e.g. How we make a decision, how childhood experiences affect decision-making, how we develop the ability to make decisions. The recorded video will be submitted via KEATS at the end of the semester. There will be opportunity to discuss ideas and gain peer feedback on video plans during seminar activities.

6PAHPAPP.information: This blended module uses a mixture of conventional (videoed content and in-person seminars) and experiential learning in small groups to develop your understanding of how teaching is developed and delivered in Higher Education. During the first few weeks of the module, you will have videoed content and in-person seminars to cover key foundational information about the Higher Education sector and key concepts in teaching and learning. You will then work in small groups in workshops and tutorials to develop a teaching resource for psychology suitable for first year undergraduates in the form of a KEATS page. You will be required to keep a record of your own development and how this relates to professional standards in education. The assessment for the module is entirely coursework with the group teaching resource accounting for 50% of the total module grade and your individual development record accounting for the remaining 50%.
6PAHPAPP.assessment:
The module assessment is entirely through coursework. You will have two components: 
• Teaching resource (group project) (50%). You will work in groups of 4-6 to produce a KEATS page for a first year Psychology module. You will begin this from Week 4 and have around 5-6 weeks to complete the work with a deadline in Week 9/10 (timetabling dependent). 
• Development/Reflection log (50%). Each week you will be asked to make a brief reflection 
(~100 words) and then after the 10 weeks you will identify three dimensions from the UKPSF 
and write up to 200 words for each to demonstrate how you have met this criterion. The deadline for this will be after teaching has completed (i.e. after Week 10).

6PAHPBEH.information:
This module will provide in-depth knowledge of the advances that have been made in behavioural genetics during recent years with the growing evidence for the role of genes in shaping our 
behaviour. Students will examine the role of environment in behaviour and gene-environment interplay (gene-environment interaction and gene-environment correlation) will be introduced. It will place a strong emphasis on enabling the students to develop critically appraisal skills of the relevant scientific literature and begin to appreciate the interdisciplinary approach used within this specialised field. The module will cover a range of mental health disorders for example anorexia nervosa, anxiety and depression and schizophrenia. The module aims to develop an understanding of the current theoretical and methodological approaches used in behavioural genetics.

6PAHPCHI.information:
This module aims to provide a comprehensive introduction to the fields of global mental health and global child development, introducing evidence, research methods and key theories associated with mental health and child development as these apply to low and middle income country (LMIC) settings. The module will cover core research methods as well as critiques of the field from the start, encouraging students to critically appraise the strength of evidence, research methods and modes of intervention development and delivery over the course of the module. The module will consider the role of culture and cultural differences throughout, with a particular focus on the tension between the export of universalist Western theories and local understandings of mental health, child development and developmental disabilities such as autism and intellectual disability.   
The module will explore mental health and child development in the context of LMIC societies and health systems, including the complex interrelationships between poverty, gender, stigma and mental health and developmental disabilities. The so-called ‘treatment gap’ between the burden of disease and the availability of services and mental health specialists needed to diagnose and support people with mental ill health or developmental disabilities will be explored, in the context of lay understandings of mental health and child development, and the role of alternative (non-biomedical) treatment systems.  
Finally, the module will examine solutions to the treatment gap, including training non-specialists to deliver mental health and early child development interventions (‘task-sharing’). Methods for psychological intervention development, evaluation and delivery will be examined and current challenges, including issues associated with scale-up, ensuring quality of care and sustainability will be explored.

6PAHPCNS.information: The Clinical Neurodevelopmental Sciences module provides a unique opportunity to explore cutting edge research into neurodevelopmental disorders and see how these findings are being translated into clinical practice. The module is taught by world leading scientists and clinicians with additional input from service user groups. Students will develop their knowledge and understanding of the clinical issues regarding the diagnosis and treatment of neurodevelopmental disorders and how research is improving detection and treatment options.
6PAHPCNS.assessment: Case report (40%), presentation (20%), group MCQs (15%), individual MCQs (25%)

6PAHPEXT.information:
This module provides students with the opportunity to study a topic of their own interest that lies outside the content covered by the taught modules they are studying. Students will work under the 
guidance/advice of a supervisor, but will have primary responsibility for developing their own specific question that their written work will address; searching, reading and analysing the literature than can be used to address that literature; and planning and preparing a long piece of writing that presents a synthesis and analysis of the literature that addresses their chosen question. The module provides students with an opportunity to develop a more specialised and higher-level of understanding of an area of psychology that they have been introduced to elsewhere in their degree, or to pursue an interest in an area of psychology which is not covered with the BSc Psychology programme. The role of the supervisor is to provide advice and support on the key ‘starting point’ for the literature, and assistance with refining the question to be addressed. Supervisors also provide constructive feedback on an early draft of the article. The extended piece of writing is submitted towards the end of the academic year, but it is recommended that students meet with their supervisor and begin working on this module in Term 1.

6PAHPMEC.module_leaders: Dr Po-Wah So & Dr Daniel van Wamelen
6PAHPMEC.information:
Collectively, neurodegenerative conditions including Alzheimer's Disease and Parkinson's Disease, 
pose a major societal challenge, especially in an aging population. We are still a considerable way 
from understanding their causes and even further from developing cures. Despite this, the growing 
body of basic science and clinical research – including biology/neuroscience and psychology – is 
being translated into novel ways to benefit patients. The module will explore a range of examples 
including how basic research can deliver useful biological and psychological markers of disease 
offering the chance of earlier detection. We will also explore areas where the evidence base is less 
solid such as whether (and why) psychological and "brain training" might be useful ways to manage 
some of the symptoms and even protect against or slow the course of the disease. 
Through a series of lectures and seminars exploring these and other developments, you will learn 
how to appraise and synthesize evidence from across a range of different discipline areas, and 
understand the opportunities and challenges of effectively translating basic research into patient 
benefit in this complex area. You will also learn how to communicate scientific evidence in a 
balanced but accessible way to a non-scientific audience with a practical workshop on blogging.

6PAHPSMH.information:
In this module students will review the evidence for the high prevalence of mental health problems among the student population and critically evaluate current approaches to understanding and addressing poor mental health. 
Data published in 2021 indicated that over 40% of young adults in Higher Education may meet criteria for a mental health problem. The traditional medical model positions the causes and treatments of mental ill-health at the level of the individual, but can our conventional one-to-one clinical approaches to treating mental health problems address the apparent scale of demand? Should we really be encouraging 40% of young university students to seek help for their mental health? 
The module will present alternative frameworks for understanding mental health, including societal and political perspectives. Through critical reflection on alternative models for understanding mental health, students will develop their own novel solutions for addressing student mental health at King's. Students will be supported to communicate their ideas through the formal structure of a business plan.

6PAHPTBB.information: This module will introduce students to a range of fundamental perspectives and issues in economic psychology and behavioural economics. The module consists of lectures that are structured around two main themes: Theories of Preference and Choice, and Economic Behaviour in Context. Through exploring key concepts from economic psychology (e.g., bounded rationality, heuristics, fairness, mental accounting), students will learn how findings and theories from psychology and economics can contribute to a convergent perspective. Besides covering major theoretical models, students will be introduced to more specific psychological principles in economic behaviour. Classic paradigms such as Trust Games, Social Dilemmas, Taboo Trade-offs, the Delta Game, the St. Petersburg Paradox, the Monty Hall Problem, and the Dollar Bet Game will be used to illustrate when and why psychological processes influence economic decision making. In addition to addressing theory and psychological processes, this module will connect research insights to consumer behaviour and phenomena with important societal impact (e.g., unemployment, sunk cost fallacy, inaction inertia, endowment effect). It is helpful for students to have a basic understanding of social psychology, social cognition, or decision making, but this is neither a requirement nor a necessity for passing the module. The module focuses on economic behaviour in the individual and small-group context.
6PAHPTBB.assessment: Weekly Tests (40%); Critical Review (35%); Group presentation (15%); Tournament (10%)

6PAHPTOP.information: In this module you will learn about recent work on consciousness in psychology, neuroscience and philosophy, exploring respects in which making progress in the scientific study of consciousness depends on synthesising insights and methods from these different disciplines. Through a combination of lectures, seminars and self-guided learning, you will learn about methodological and foundational problems related to questions such as the following: Do scientific theories and experiments enable us to locate the neural correlates of consciousness, or to characterise the mechanisms of consciousness in terms of information processing? What is the difference between consciousness and implicit or non-conscious processing, and how can experiments distinguish between them? Does consciousness play a distinctive role in explaining human action? What theoretical assumptions about the nature of consciousness must we make in order to answer the questions above one way or another?Each week, there is an article to read in one of the three disciplines, a prerecorded lecture to watch in your own time (60-80 minutes) and a one-hour in-person discussion seminar.
6PAHPTOP.assessment: Critical review of a core reading (25%), Interdisciplinary Essay (75%)

6PAHPWOR.name: Occupational Psychology
6PAHPWOR.information:
The aim of this module is to introduce students to principles of work and organisational psychology. The module will be taught via a series of lectures, practical work and small groups seminars where 
lecture topics will be examined in greater depth. This module will provide students with an insight into professional practices of work and organisational psychology.  Students will learn about evidence based practice and in work organisational psychology, including how psychologists utilise theory and methods to inform practice. Students will be introduced to a variety of pertinent organisational topics and will explore these at an individual, group and system level. 

The module aims to cover key concepts, methods and insights from work and organisational 
psychology to illustrate them in relation to professional practice issues. The module will enable students to apply the methods and rigour of psychology to issues important to business in order to advance understanding of individual, group and organisational factors in relation to business effectiveness.

6PAHTMPR.information: The aim of this module is to provide a detailed introduction to psycholinguistics. We will introduce some of the most influential psycholinguistic models of speaking, listening, reading, and writing, and guide students towards being able to critically evaluate the strength and weaknesses of these models in light of the evidence provided by contemporary psycholinguistic research. The module will also cover some of the key research methods employed in this area of research and discuss to what extent these methods help us make meaningful inferences about language and cognition. Furthermore, the module will cover the complexities involved in different forms of communication, including bilingual language processing, sign language, and metacommunication during play.
6PAHTMPR.assessment: The module will be assessed through one 2500-word coursework assignment (75%) and one short (e.g., 5 minute) presentation (25%). The assignment will ask students to write a short book chapter to appear in a psycholinguistics collection with the title such as: ‘[Combination of two topics]: The link between theory and research explained’, including an abstract and highlights for a lay audience. They will also be asked to create a short TED-style talk with a specified title such as ‘Five things you need to know about X’ where X is one of the lecture topics. This will be presented to their peers during the final two weeks of term. Weekly formative quizzes presented at the beginning of the in-person sessions will contain 5-10 questions. They will focus on evaluating the students’ understanding of the link between the theory and the research they learned in the previous week. Topics: Introduction to psycholinguistic theory and research; Speaking; Listening; Reading and writing; The neuroscience of language; Bilingualism and second language acquisition; Speech and Language Therapy; Language development; Metacommunication during play; Language in the social world; Sign language (bonus topic).

6PAHTTTP.module_leaders: Dr Julia Ouzia & Dr Maria Livanou
6PAHTTTP.information: The aim of this module is to provide an overview of the key approaches to counselling and psychotherapy through a contemporary lens. We will introduce historical perspectives deriving from psychodynamic, humanistic, and cognitive-behavioural traditions and discuss how they inform contemporary therapeutic practice. Furthermore, we will discuss important issues such as ethical practice, working with diversity, working with families and vulnerable groups (e.g., young people, older adults, clients with psychiatric diagnoses), and explore the impact of these issues on therapeutic work. Finally, we will consider debates in the field of counselling and psychotherapy and the ways in which research can address these.
6PAHTTTP.assessment: Coursework Transcript commentary 1000 words (50%) with formative submission opportunity & Reflective commentary 1000 words (50%)

6PAHUEDP.information: This module, Understanding Eating Disorders: Pathways from Onset to Recovery, aims to provide a thorough exploration of eating disorders by focusing on their clinical presentation, causes, impact, treatment, and recovery pathways. Students will develop an understanding of various eating disorders, including anorexia nervosa, bulimia nervosa, and avoidant restrictive food intake disorder, while examining their biological, psychological, and sociocultural influences. The module will also explore the far-reaching effects of these disorders on mental, physical, and social well-being, as well as their emotional impact on families and caregivers. Through the study of evidence-based treatments—such as cognitive-behavioural therapy, family-based therapy, and emerging approaches—students will evaluate strategies for effective intervention and holistic care. Additional topics include the influence of social and medical stigma alongside social media on body image and eating disorders, as well as the legal and ethical considerations in treatment and current debates. Emphasising the complexities of recovery, the module will cover relapse prevention, resilience building, and the critical role of support systems, with real-world case studies and lived experience recovery stories to deepen understanding.
6PAHUEDP.assessment: The module assessment will consist of two coursework assignments. The first assignment is a 1,000-word reflective account on students' learning from the first five weeks of the module, structured around two key components. To support this, students will maintain a weekly reflective log, writing up to 100 words per week in either a blog-style format or a private weekly log accessible only to the lecturer. This log will serve as an ongoing reflective journal, allowing students to document their thoughts on each session, engage critically with course materials, and integrate insights from their studies. Formative feedback will be provided in Week 3 to help students refine their reflections and stay on track for the summative assessment due in Week 6. Drawing from their weekly entries, this log will act as an ongoing reflective journal, allowing students to explore new concepts, critically engage with course materials, and prepare for their final coursework. Students will reflect in the weekly logs on how the material covered affects their understanding of eating disorders and their views on treatment and prevention. Based on their journal entries, they will write a reflective essay that synthesises their learning experiences and personal insights. This essay will illustrate how the knowledge gained from the module has influenced their perspectives on eating disorders and how it will impact their future professional practice. For the second coursework assessment, students will design a community outreach project titled"Breaking the Silence: Understanding Eating Disorders," due in Week 11. This project aims to raise awareness about eating disorders within the local community through educational initiatives and interactive engagement. As part of the project, students will develop informative materials such as pamphlets, leaflets, posters, or a PowerPoint presentation, covering key topics including different types of eating disorders, their signs and symptoms, and available support resources. Additionally, students may propose organising informational talks or workshops featuring healthcare professionals and individuals with lived experience to encourage open discussions about mental health and body image. The final submission will be a 10-page report outlining the project’s objectives, content, and impact. To support students in their preparation, a dedicated seminar and an optional drop-in session will be provided.

6ZZZAPPS.information:
This module will build on 5PAH0333 Psychology of Human Performance and Expertise. However, you may still select this module if you have not taken the 5PAH0333. You may need to conduct extra 
reading in some weeks if you have not taken the level 5 module, but we have had many successful students take this module on it’s own.  In 6ZZZAPPS Applied Performance Psychology, we will dig deeper into the real issues you will face in work in high performance settings. We will explore how understanding the psychological underpinnings of performance can be applied through both client-facing work to enhance performance and through applied research. We will discuss the opportunities that answering applied research questions presents and the challenges faced when trying to conduct research in real world performance settings. We will also critically evaluate interventions, how these should be delivered, and overview the skills you would require working as a performance psychologist.  There will be eight lectures and tutorials delivered by the module lead and guest lecturers who specialise in a specific applied performance domain. You will also have two weeks of supervised learning where you learn to solve real world performance problems through research and develop skills needed to work in performance environments.
